fragments.content: Neuromodulation uses electrical or magnetic stimulation to alter neural activity for therapeutic purposes. Deep brain stimulation (DBS): surgically implanted electrodes deliver high-frequency stimulation to specific brain targets. FDA-approved indications: Parkinson's disease (subthalamic nucleus or GPi targets—improves motor fluctuations, dyskinesias, tremor), essential tremor (VIM thalamus—reduces tremor), dystonia (GPi—especially generalized/segmental dystonia), OCD (anterior limb of internal capsule—refractory cases). Investigational: depression (subcallosal cingulate, VC/VS), epilepsy (ANT thalamus—approved for focal epilepsy), Tourette syndrome, addiction. Mechanism: high-frequency stimulation inhibits pathological neuronal firing, modulates network activity. Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS): non-invasive magnetic coil over scalp induces electrical currents in cortex. FDA-approved: major depressive disorder (left DLPFC—rTMS; bilateral—theta burst), OCD (medial prefrontal/anterior cingulate), smoking cessation, migraine with aura (single-pulse TMS). Vagus nerve stimulation (VNS): implanted pulse generator stimulates left vagus nerve—approved for refractory epilepsy and treatment-resistant depression.

Neuromodulation requires careful patient selection and programming. DBS for Parkinson's: candidates must have idiopathic PD with good levodopa response but disabling motor fluctuations/dyskinesias despite optimal medical therapy; exclude significant cognitive impairment, psychiatric disease, atypical parkinsonism. Surgical procedure: stereotactic electrode placement (awake craniotomy with microelectrode recording or asleep under general anesthesia with intraoperative imaging), IPG implantation in chest. Programming begins 2-4 weeks post-op: adjust amplitude, frequency (typically 130-185 Hz), pulse width to optimize benefit/minimize side effects (paresthesias, muscle tightening, visual phenomena, speech/balance problems). Multiple contacts allow directional steering. Battery life 3-5 years (non-rechargeable) or 9-15 years (rechargeable). TMS for depression: daily sessions (5 days/week × 4-6 weeks), left DLPFC high-frequency (10Hz, 120% motor threshold, 3000 pulses/session) or right DLPFC low-frequency (1Hz) or bilateral theta burst (intermittent 50Hz bursts—shorter session time, non-inferior efficacy). Side effects: scalp discomfort, headache, rare seizure (<0.1%). Contraindications: metal implants near coil, seizure disorder (relative), pacemaker/ICD (relative). VNS: gradual titration over months; cough/voice changes common initially.

Neuromodulation candidate evaluation requires multidisciplinary assessment. DBS Parkinson's: confirm idiopathic PD (UK Brain Bank criteria), document levodopa response (>30% UPDRS-III improvement in acute challenge), assess motor complications (wearing-off, dyskinesia duration/severity), cognitive screening (MoCA ≥26 preferred, formal neuropsychological testing if borderline), psychiatric evaluation (exclude active depression, psychosis, impulse control disorders), MRI brain (exclude significant atrophy, vascular burden, structural lesions). Essential tremration: quantify tremor severity ( Bain scale), exclude reversible causes (thyroid, medications). Dystonia: genetic testing if early-onset (DYT1), assess distribution (generalized/segmental better candidates than focal). TMS depression: confirm MDD diagnosis (DSM-5), document treatment resistance (failure ≥1-2 adequate antidepressant trials), exclude contraindications (metallic implants within 30cm of coil, cochlear implant, history of seizures), screen for bipolar disorder (risk of mania). Outcome measures: UPDRS-III for PD, Fahn-Tolosa-Marin for tremor, Burke-Fahn-Marsden for dystonia, HAM-D/MADRS for depression, seizure frequency for epilepsy. Realistic expectations crucial—DBS improves symptoms but doesn't halt disease progression.
